steps.instruction: Read this short paragraph. Notice how the ideas feel choppy.
steps.work: Recess is important. Kids need to move their bodies. Kids need a break from sitting. Recess helps kids focus when they go back to class.
steps.explanation: The sentences all support the main idea, but they feel disconnected. Linking words will help connect them smoothly.

steps.instruction: Pick linking words that show how the ideas connect. Try: also, for example, because, another reason.
steps.work: Linking words to try: 'For one thing,' 'Also,' 'Because of this,'
steps.explanation: Different linking words do different jobs. 'For one thing' introduces a first reason. 'Also' adds another reason. 'Because of this' shows a result.

steps.instruction: Rewrite the paragraph with linking words at the start of supporting sentences.
steps.work: Recess is important. For one thing, kids need to move their bodies. Also, kids need a break from sitting. Because of this, recess helps kids focus when they go back to class.
steps.explanation: The linking words connect the ideas. The paragraph flows much better now.

steps.instruction: Read the paragraph aloud. Do the transitions make sense?
steps.work: Yes! 'For one thing' introduces the first reason, 'Also' adds another, and 'Because of this' shows the result.
steps.explanation: Good transitions guide the reader from one idea to the next. If a transition sounds odd when you read aloud, try a different one.

title: Using Transitions to Show Time Order

steps.instruction: Read this paragraph about making a sandwich. Notice the missing connections.
steps.work: You can make a peanut butter sandwich. Get two slices of bread. Spread peanut butter on one slice. Put the other slice on top.
steps.explanation: The steps are in the right order, but without linking words, it is hard to follow the sequence.

steps.instruction: Pick words that show the order of steps. Try: first, next, then, finally.
steps.work: Time-order words: First, Next, Then, Finally
steps.explanation: Time-order transitions help the reader follow steps in sequence. They are great for how-to writing.

steps.instruction: Rewrite the paragraph with time-order transitions.
steps.work: You can make a peanut butter sandwich. First, get two slices of bread. Next, spread peanut butter on one slice. Finally, put the other slice on top.
steps.explanation: 'First,' 'Next,' and 'Finally' make the steps clear and easy to follow.

steps.instruction: Read the paragraph. Can you follow the steps in order?
steps.work: Yes! The transitions make the order clear: First get bread, next spread, finally put together.
steps.explanation: Time-order transitions (first, next, then, after that, finally) are linking words that help readers follow a sequence of events or steps.
